metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the AC how `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks` reaches the `_model_override is None` code path offline (e.g., 'construct the runner with `model_override=None` and patch `_run_agent_call` to return a zero-usage `RunResult` stub directly, bypassing the real HTTP call').
findings.finding: The `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks` AC requires asserting a WARNING via `caplog` on 'an all-zero-usage successful run', but the warning is gated on `self._model_override is None` (offline test doubles must NEVER warn, per the plan). The plan does not specify how the test achieves a successful run with `_model_override is None` without making a live API call — leaving the executor to either (a) make an unmocked live call (expensive/fragile), (b) patch internals inconsistently, or (c) accidentally write a test that satisfies the AC while the `_model_override is None` guard causes the warning to never fire, making the test a false positive.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING; AC `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'Test setup: instantiate runner with `model_override=None`; patch `runner._run_agent_call` (or equivalent internal) to return a synthetic `RunResult` with `usage.input_tokens=0, output_tokens=0, requests=1`; assert WARNING in caplog; assert no exception raised. This exercises the guard branch directly without a live API call.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete cache-key derivation expression in the plan (e.g., `getattr(model, 'model_name', repr(model))`) so `test_capability_check_cached` can assert the correct identity semantics.
findings.finding: The cache key for `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` is described as 'keyed by the resolved model string' but the check runs on a model OBJECT (not a string path). The plan never specifies how the string key is derived from the object (e.g., `model.model_name`, `str(model)`, `repr(model)`). An incorrect key would silently break the 'once per unique model per process' guarantee without any AC catching it.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; AC `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check' bullet: 'cache key = `getattr(model, 'model_name', None) or repr(model)`; `test_capability_check_cached` asserts the key is present in `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` after one call and that a second call with the same model object does not access `model.profile` again (verified via `unittest.mock.patch.object` on the profile property or a call counter on the helper).'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` is keyed by the `resolved` string (the provider-qualified model string computed before object construction, e.g. `'anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6'`) — not by any attribute of the constructed model object — so the cache key is deterministic and deduplicated across calls with the same model.
findings.finding: The cache key for `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` is described as 'keyed by the resolved model string,' but the plan places the capability check in `runner.py` where, on the anthropic path, the model OBJECT is constructed first (via `_build_retrying_anthropic_model`, line ~251) and then the check would operate on the object. The `resolved` string (line ~224) is available in scope before object construction, but the plan never explicitly states that `resolved` — not `str(model)`, `type(model).__name__`, or any derived attribute — is the cache key. If an implementer keys on the model object rather than `resolved`, the cache never hits (every call constructs a new object) and the 'once per model per process' invariant silently fails.
findings.location: What — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; CACHE description
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add: 'The cache key is `resolved` — the provider-qualified model string (e.g. `anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6`) computed by `_pai_model(cfg)` before any object construction. The set membership check (`resolved in _TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED`) and add (`_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED.add(resolved)`) both reference this string, never the model object.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact cache key for `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` — e.g. 'the `resolved` string (provider-qualified model string already computed by `_pai_model(cfg)` before object construction) is used as the key' — and reference it in the `test_capability_check_cached` AC.
findings.finding: The `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` module-level set cache key is described as 'keyed by the resolved model string' but the check operates on a model OBJECT (the `AnthropicModel` instance); the plan never specifies how the string key is derived from the object, leaving the implementation ambiguously specified.
findings.location: What section ('Construction-time provider tool-calling check'), AC line: `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'The cache key is the `resolved` provider-qualified model string (e.g. `"anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6"`) already computed by `_pai_model(cfg)` prior to object construction — not a property of the model object itself.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test-isolation strategy for `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` in the plan (e.g., a pytest fixture that clears or monkeypatches the module-level set before each relevant test) so cache-state does not leak between tests.
findings.finding: The module-level `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` set persists across tests in the same process, yet the plan specifies no test-isolation strategy (e.g., clearing or patching the set in each test's setup/teardown). Tests in `test_agent_build_invariants.py` that depend on a cache miss (e.g., `test_capability_check_fails_fast`, `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model`) may silently pass or fail depending on test execution order.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — `test_capability_check_cached`, `test_capability_check_fails_fast`, `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model`; Files: `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `autouse` fixture in `test_agent_build_invariants.py` that does `rebar.llm.runner._TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED.clear()` (or uses `monkeypatch.setattr`) before each test in the module. Reference this fixture in the AC for `test_capability_check_cached` and `test_capability_check_fails_fast`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pytest fixture (e.g. `autouse=True` in the test file or an explicit fixture parameter) that clears `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` before each capability-check test, so each test starts with an empty cache and the 'first call uncached / second call cached' assertion in `test_capability_check_cached` is reliably exercised.
findings.finding: The module-level `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` set is a process-lifetime mutable singleton with no specified test-isolation or reset mechanism. Tests that exercise the capability check (`test_capability_check_fails_fast`, `test_capability_check_cached`) will leave their model strings in the set for the duration of the test process. A subsequent test that tries to exercise the 'uncached' code path for the same model string will silently find it already cached and skip the check — making `test_capability_check_cached`'s counter assertion trivially pass for the wrong reason, and making it impossible to reliably re-exercise the fail-fast path in the same process.
findings.location: What — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; AC — `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC section or the Files section, add: 'Each capability-check test must use a fixture that calls `runner._TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED.clear()` in setup (via `monkeypatch.setattr` or direct mutation + teardown), so tests are order-independent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `requests > 0` as an explicit condition in the `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks` AC so the test exercises the full three-part gate (`input_tokens==0 AND output_tokens==0 AND requests>0`) and also asserts no warning fires when `requests==0`.
findings.finding: The usage-plausibility warning decision rule contains a `requests > 0` guard in the plan body that is absent from the corresponding AC, creating a split specification: an implementer reading only the AC could omit the guard and false-positive on single-turn runs that naturally report zero requests.
findings.location: What section ('Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING') vs AC line: `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks`
findings.suggested_fix: Update the AC line to: 'Usage-plausibility emits a WARNING (asserted via caplog) on an all-zero-usage successful run WITH requests>0; asserts NO warning when requests==0 (single-turn / no model call made).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding test case, e.g. `test_capability_check_noop_for_model_override`) that asserts no exception is raised and no profile introspection occurs when `model_override` is set, covering the explicitly described model_override no-op guarantee.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly describes the model_override no-op path as a concrete behavioral guarantee ('the `model_override` test double is NEVER checked, concretely and by construction') but no AC verifies this property. Only `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model` is listed; there is no `test_capability_check_noop_for_model_override` (or equivalent) AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — capability check AC block; What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC block: '[ ] Construction-time check: when `self._model_override` is set (test-double path), the capability check is entirely skipped and no `LLMConfigError` is raised, even if the override object lacks a `.profile` — `test_capability_check_noop_for_model_override`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and implement an atomicity mechanism for `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` mutations (e.g., a `threading.Lock` wrapping the check-and-add block, or accept the benign-double-run explicitly in the plan and relax the `test_capability_check_cached` assertion to 'at most once per model string per thread' rather than 'exactly once per process').
findings.finding: The module-level `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` set has a check-then-act TOCTOU race: any caller can observe `model_str not in _TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED`, then two concurrent threads both proceed to run the capability check and both call `set.add()` before either has added the entry. The plan claims the check runs 'once per unique model per process' but specifies no atomicity mechanism.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; AC line `test_capability_check_cached`; Files: `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the check-then-add block with a module-level `threading.Lock`: acquire lock → if already in set, return → run profile check → add to set → release. Alternatively, document in the plan that double-execution is benign (the check is idempotent) and update `test_capability_check_cached` to assert ≥1 rather than exactly 1 profile access.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Narrow `test_expected_tools_offered` to cover only `filesystem_tools` tool names (the gap not yet covered), and cross-reference `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` for rebar_tools — or explicitly note why a second assertion is needed (e.g. to verify the combined list after concatenation in `runner.py:218`).
findings.finding: The planned `test_expected_tools_offered` for `rebar_tools` tool names rebuilds coverage that already exists verbatim in `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py:274-278`, which asserts `[t.__name__ for t in read_only] == ['show_ticket']` and `{t.__name__ for t in full} == {'show_ticket', 'comment_ticket'}`. Adding this same assertion in a new file is redundant duplication (NIH / Rule-of-Three violation in reverse).
findings.location: AC — 'Test: pai_tools.filesystem_tools/rebar_tools offer exactly the expected tool names — fails if a tool is stripped (test_expected_tools_offered)'; Tests section — 'tools actually offered'
findings.suggested_fix: In the new test file, assert only `{t.__name__ for t in pai_tools.filesystem_tools('.')} == {'read_file', 'list_directory', 'search_files'}`, pointing to the existing `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` for rebar_tools coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sanity-assertion test (independent of the capability-check logic) that directly imports `pydantic_ai.models.anthropic.AnthropicModel`, constructs one offline, and asserts `hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` — so a wrong attribute path fails the test suite loudly rather than silently making the guard a permanent no-op.
findings.finding: `model.profile.supports_tools` is asserted as a 'VERIFIED pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal' — the entire construction-time capability check rests on this attribute — yet Grep across the whole codebase returns zero hits for `supports_tools` or `model.profile`. The only evidence is the plan's own inline experiment log, which is self-referential. If this attribute does not exist in the installed version (e.g. renamed, behind a different namespace, or absent before a certain minor), the check silently becomes a no-op (the defensive `getattr(..., None)` pattern means a wrong attribute name never raises — it just always reads `None`, which the plan counts as a safe skip, meaning the guard never fires on ANY model, defeating its purpose).
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; Verified library behavior section
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_agent_build_invariants.py`, add a dedicated test `test_anthropic_model_profile_attribute_exists` that constructs an `AnthropicModel` offline (no API key needed for construction) and asserts `isinstance(model.profile.supports_tools, bool)`. This pins the library contract independently of the capability-check logic, so a pydantic-ai rename fails loudly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the `test_expected_tools_offered` AC, acknowledge that `rebar_tools.__name__` assertions already exist in `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` (test_pydantic_ai_runner.py:274–278) and specify whether the new test should extend that existing test, cross-reference it, or only add the `filesystem_tools` `__name__` half.
findings.finding: The plan's `test_expected_tools_offered` AC specifies asserting `__name__`s on both `pai_tools.filesystem_tools` AND `pai_tools.rebar_tools` in the new file, but `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py::test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` (lines 277–278) already asserts exactly those `rebar_tools` `__name__`s (`['show_ticket']` / `{'show_ticket', 'comment_ticket'}`). The plan does not acknowledge this existing coverage, leaving the implementer to either silently duplicate the rebar_tools assertion (creating drift risk between two canonical sources) or inadvertently omit it from the new test with no guidance.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `test_expected_tools_offered` / `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py` lines 274–278
findings.suggested_fix: Narrow `test_expected_tools_offered` to assert only `filesystem_tools`'s `__name__`s (`['read_file', 'list_directory', 'search_files']`) since that's the genuinely new coverage, and add a comment referencing `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` as the canonical source for `rebar_tools` names.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a primary-source citation for `model.profile.supports_tools` on pydantic-ai `AnthropicModel` — a pydantic-ai docs URL, GitHub source permalink (e.g. `pydantic_ai/models/anthropic.py`), or changelog entry — to resolve the open question the repo's own research doc flagged ('Verify the current `pydantic_ai.profiles` entry for Anthropic') before committing to this as the check mechanism.
findings.finding: The plan's central capability assertion — that `model.profile.supports_tools` is a reliable bool attribute on a pydantic-ai 1.107.0 model object — is supported only by an inline experiment log with no external anchor. The repo's own structured-output research doc (line 54) says Anthropic profile support 'is resolved through pydantic_ai.profiles' and directs implementers to 'Verify the current pydantic_ai.profiles entry for Anthropic'. structured.py lines 40–46 also marks this as unconfirmed. Zero codebase uses of `model.profile`, `ModelProfile`, or `supports_tools` exist. The plan's parenthetical disclaimer ('a code-grounding pass must not read repo-absence as non-existence') does not resolve the absence of any cited external primary source (pydantic-ai docs URL, GitHub source line, changelog entry).
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; Verified library behavior section
findings.suggested_fix: Pin a pydantic-ai source permalink such as `https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-ai/blob/v1.107.0/pydantic_ai_slim/pydantic_ai/models/anthropic.py` and confirm the `.profile.supports_tools` attribute exists there. If it does not, fall back to the already-working `AnthropicModel` isinstance-check or the model string prefix, both of which are confirmed in the codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Anchor `model.profile.supports_tools` to a specific pydantic-ai sub-version floor (e.g. add `pydantic-ai-slim>=1.107.0` to the `agents` extra, or cite the public changelog/source line where `ModelProfile.supports_tools` was introduced) so the construction-time guard cannot silently become a no-op under a within-range pip upgrade.
findings.finding: `model.profile.supports_tools` is the plan's central new API surface — the entire construction-time capability check is built on it — but it has zero codebase hits and its only evidence is the plan's own inline experiment log ('ACTUAL captured output'). The version constraint in pyproject.toml is `pydantic-ai-slim>=1,<2`, a wide range; if `.profile` was added mid-v1.x, a pip upgrade within that range could silently make the whole guard a no-op (the defensive `getattr` passes through `None`, so `supports is False` never fires).
findings.location: ## What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; ## Verified library behavior (experiment log — pydantic-ai 1.107.0, offline)
findings.suggested_fix: In pyproject.toml `agents` extra, tighten the floor to `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic,retries]>=1.107.0,<2`. Also add a `test_capability_check_noop_for_missing_profile` assertion that confirms a model object with NO `.profile` attribute correctly skips (not crashes), and separately document that this is the intended safe-skip-not-guard behavior so a reader understands the no-op path is intentional.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC test (e.g. `test_capability_check_skips_on_missing_profile`) that asserts the construction-time check is a no-op (does not raise) when the model object has no `.profile` attribute or `.profile.supports_tools` is `None`.
findings.finding: The decision rule for the `supports_tools` guard handles three value states (True, False, None) but the AC omits a test for the `None`/missing-profile case, leaving the 'safe skip on missing profile' fallback unspecified in the acceptance criteria.
findings.location: What section ('Construction-time provider tool-calling check') vs AC lines `test_capability_check_fails_fast` / `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model`
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC block: '[ ] Construction-time check: a model OBJECT whose `.profile` is absent or whose `.profile.supports_tools` is `None` is a NO-OP — no raise, no log (`test_capability_check_skips_on_missing_profile`).'

findings.finding: The plan's central construction-time capability check is built entirely on `model.profile.supports_tools` — claimed as a 'VERIFIED pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal' — but there is zero evidence of this attribute in any repo file (codebase search returns no hits), no external documentation URL, no committed POC snippet, and no pydantic-ai source pointer. The only support is the plan's own inline experiment log, which cannot be independently verified.
findings.location: ## What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; ## Verified library behavior (experiment log — pydantic-ai 1.107.0, offline)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's Verified library behavior section: a pydantic-ai source reference (e.g. `pydantic_ai/models/__init__.py` `ModelProfile` class) and/or a two-line reproducible snippet: `from pydantic_ai.models.anthropic import AnthropicModel; m = AnthropicModel('claude-opus-4-8', ...); assert hasattr(m, 'profile') and hasattr(m.profile, 'supports_tools')`.

findings.finding: `model.profile.supports_tools` is asserted as a 'VERIFIED pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal' — the entire construction-time capability check is built on this attribute — but there are zero hits for `profile` or `supports_tools` anywhere in the rebar codebase (no import, no usage, no test). The only evidence is the plan's own inline experiment log, making this empirically unverifiable from the repo.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; AC: `test_capability_check_fails_fast`

Also cite the specific pydantic-ai source file/line (e.g. `pydantic_ai/models/anthropic.py`) in the plan's verified-behavior log.

findings.finding: The plan's central capability assertion — that `model.profile.supports_tools` is a reliable bool attribute on a pydantic-ai `AnthropicModel` object in version 1.107.0 — is cited only from a self-contained inline experiment log with no external anchor (no GitHub source URL, no API docs link, no changelog reference). There are zero hits for `supports_tools` or `model.profile` anywhere in the codebase. This is the sole mechanism behind the construction-time guard; a wrong attribute name silently makes the check a permanent NO-OP (the plan's own `hasattr` guard suppresses AttributeError).
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; Verified library behavior section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the test file: `from pydantic_ai.models.anthropic import AnthropicModel; m = AnthropicModel('claude-3-haiku-20240307', provider=...); assert hasattr(m, 'profile') and isinstance(m.profile.supports_tools, bool)` — this pins the attribute contract and fails loudly if pydantic-ai renames it.

findings.finding: `model.profile.supports_tools` is asserted as a 'VERIFIED pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal' — the entire construction-time capability check is built on this attribute — yet there is zero codebase usage of `.profile` on a model object, and the codebase's own structured.py (lines 40–46) explicitly defers depending on the pydantic-ai model profile, commenting it is 'until the profile is confirmed.' The evidence is the plan's own inline experiment log with no external verifiable anchor (no PyPI docs URL, no GitHub source citation, no changelog reference).
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; Verified library behavior section; AC: `test_capability_check_fails_fast`

If the attribute is absent, name the actual bool signal that pydantic-ai does expose for tool-calling capability (e.g. check the `ModelProfile` dataclass source in pydantic-ai for the correct field name).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'spy vs. counter' fork in `test_capability_check_cached` by stating the preferred approach (e.g., 'add an integer `_capability_check_count` to the helper, increment on each profile access, assert it equals 1 after two identical-model calls').
findings.finding: The AC for `test_capability_check_cached` contains a fork — 'spies on the `model.profile` access — or a counter in the check helper' — leaving the implementation strategy unspecified. Both approaches produce different test shapes (one requires patching a property, the other requires a module-level counter), and the executor must make a real design choice that the plan defers.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '— or a counter in the check helper —' with a single stated approach: 'a module-level `_capability_check_call_count` counter incremented inside the check helper; test asserts it equals 1 after two calls with the same model string.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the 'construction-time invariants, not per-call' architectural decision in a CLAUDE.md note, inline doc-comment in runner.py, or a brief ADR so the rationale survives code review and future edits.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — 'invariants belong in construction-time checks + tests, not per-call runtime' — that has no corresponding documentation update (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc entry). Future contributors modifying runner.py will lack context for why the module-level cache and construction-time guard exist and why per-call checking was rejected.
findings.location: ## Scope / Files: `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring block or comment in runner.py above `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` explaining: (1) why these checks are at construction time, (2) why per-call checking was rejected, and (3) what the cache key represents. Optionally add a one-liner in CLAUDE.md under an 'Architecture decisions' section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or justify `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` cache: either cite a profiling/scale result, or simplify to a plain inline check (a `getattr` check inside `run()` has negligible cost compared with the LLM call that immediately follows).
findings.finding: The `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` module-level set is unjustified as a performance optimization: the only cited reason is 'a hot loop pays nothing,' but agent construction in `runner.py` is paired 1-to-1 with a real LLM network call (the entire `run()` body), making the `getattr` cache trivially insignificant. No profiling result, call-rate estimate, or scale evidence is provided, so G-9's small-scale default forbids the optimization claim.
findings.location: What section — 'CACHE: a module-level set() _TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED keyed by the resolved model string — the check runs ONCE per unique model per process (not per call), so a hot loop pays nothing.'
findings.suggested_fix: Drop the module-level set and perform the `getattr(getattr(model, 'profile', None), 'supports_tools', None)` check inline in `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` (or in `_pai_check_config`) without caching — no observable performance difference, no singleton to reset in tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the new provider tool-calling check should be incorporated into the existing `_pai_check_config` function (runner.py:625) rather than introduced as a parallel cached mechanism — if a separate function is intentional, document why.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a parallel construction-time validation structure (`_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` + new inline check in `runner.py`) alongside the existing `_pai_check_config` seam (runner.py:625–637), which is already called at both `preflight()` (line 167) and `run()` (line 184). Adding a second, independent check path rather than extending `_pai_check_config` creates two divergent construction-time validation code paths — a premature-abstraction smell.
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check' and Files section — 'src/rebar/llm/runner.py (the cached construction-time capability check)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add the `supports_tools` check as an additional branch inside `_pai_check_config`, keyed on `tools` being non-empty and `model` being an object (not a string). This keeps all construction-time validation co-located in one place.

findings.finding: The usage-plausibility WARNING (input_tokens==0 AND output_tokens==0 AND non-empty result) will trigger on every offline test that uses `model_override` (FunctionModel/TestModel), because `_extract_usage` already returns all-zero dicts for test models that carry no real usage. This produces spurious WARNINGs in the normal test suite and would require caplog noise filtering in tests not related to this invariant.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING' section; also runner.py _extract_usage (lines 574-605)
findings.suggested_fix: In the post-run plausibility check in runner.py, wrap it: `if self._model_override is None and usage.get('input_tokens', 0) == 0 and usage.get('output_tokens', 0) == 0 and <non_empty_result>: logger.warning(...)`. This matches the guard pattern already used for `assert_gated` and the anthropic model construction.

findings.finding: The capability-check guard expression `hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` as specified in the plan evaluates `model.profile` as a Python sub-expression before `hasattr` can protect it. If any non-standard model adapter exposes a `.profile` property that raises on access (AttributeError or otherwise), the guard itself crashes rather than gracefully skipping the check. This is a structural gap in the specified decision rule for the guard protocol.
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; guard expression `not isinstance(model, str) and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` with `hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` in the plan's guard expression.

findings.finding: The plan's construction-time guard expression `hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` accesses `model.profile` as a Python sub-expression before `hasattr` can protect it. If any non-string model object lacks a `.profile` attribute (or exposes it as a property that raises a non-AttributeError exception), the guard itself throws unexpectedly rather than silently no-oping, potentially crashing agent construction for any future or third-party model type not validated against pydantic-ai 1.107.0.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`
findings.suggested_fix:
Change the guard from:
```python
not isinstance(model, str) and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')
```
to:
```python
not isinstance(model, str) and hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')
```
This makes the entire check a guaranteed no-op when the profile shape is absent, regardless of future library changes.

findings.finding: `model.profile.supports_tools` is asserted as a 'VERIFIED pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal' but has zero codebase usage and the only evidence is the plan's own inline experiment log — no external anchor (source-code path, GitHub link, or importable symbol) is cited. The pyproject.toml pins `pydantic-ai-slim>=1,<2` (not `==1.107.0`), so the attribute's existence must hold across the entire `>=1,<2` range for the construction-time check to be safe; a missing or renamed attribute on any in-range version would cause an `AttributeError` crash on the Anthropic path (not a graceful `LLMConfigError`).
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; AC: `test_capability_check_fails_fast` / `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model` / `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: In the implementation, wrap the attribute access defensively: `if hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools') and model.profile.supports_tools is False: raise LLMConfigError(...)` — this already matches the plan's stated guard condition (`hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')`), but make sure the plan's AC tests also verify the `AttributeError`-safe path (i.e. a model object with no `.profile` attribute is a no-op, not a crash). Additionally, cite a concrete import path for `model.profile` from the installed pydantic-ai package to nail the evidence class to Verified.

findings.finding: The AC for `test_capability_check_cached` says the check is 'invoked once per model string, asserted by a spy' but does not specify WHAT the spy instruments — which function or method is patched, and what call-count assertion is made. An executor must invent the spy target and the assertion form.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `test_capability_check_cached`

findings.finding: The model_override (test-double) exclusion from the construction-time capability check relies on an implicit mechanism not stated in the plan. The plan says the check is a NO-OP for `model_override` but the described guard `not isinstance(model, str)` is TRUE for any pydantic-ai TestModel (which is not a string), so the check body WOULD be entered for a test double. The only thing that would save it is TestModel lacking a `.profile` attribute (the `hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` guard), but the plan presents the NO-OP as a structural property of the check, not as an implicit hasattr fallback. The plan needs to specify an explicit structural exclusion (e.g. `self._model_override is None`) as part of the guard condition, or acknowledge that `.profile` absence on TestModel is the intended exclusion mechanism.
findings.location: What section – Construction-time provider tool-calling check; AC line `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model`
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the guard description to: `self._model_override is None and not isinstance(model, str) and hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` — making the model_override exclusion structural and explicit, independent of whether the test double's type ever gains a `.profile`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
